coredata.dc:title: Conjugated carbon quantum dots: Potent nano-antibiotic for intracellular pathogens
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ardekani, Sara Madadi
Dehghani, Alireza
Ye, Ping
Nguyen, Ky-Anh
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description: A common theme in the persistence of microbial infections involves intracellular survival of microbial pathogens within host cells where they stay sheltered from attack by antimicrobial agents. In order to improve antimicrobial access inside host cells, we developed nanoparticles intracellular delivery of antibiotics. Using an intracellular infection model with the periodontal pathogen, Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis), we demonstrated significantly enhanced intracellular microbicidal activity with the standard antibiotic metronidazole (MET) through its conjugation onto 1–5 nm biocompatible nano-carrier, carbon quantum dot, which was derived from chlorophyll (cCQD). The conjugated cCQD-MET were rapidly internalized into the cultured cells, reaching almost 90% uptake within 3 h of the challenge. Our results consistently showed enhanced antimicrobial activity of the conjugate compared to MET alone. Even at concentrations as low as 0.26 µM, the conjugate showed 72% enhancement compared to the drug alone, resulting in significantly increased inhibition of intracellular P. gingivalis at lower antibiotic dosages. We achieved a high drug payload (80% w/w) on cCQD without affecting the potency of metronidazole as determined by cytotoxicity assays, cellular uptake of metronidazole, P. gingivalis invasion and elimination assays. The synthesized cCQD also displayed high fluorescence with 56% quantum yield at an absorbance peak of 380 nm and an emission peak of 480 nm, thus, allowing for fluorescence tracking and quantification of the drug intracellularly. A similar strategy may be used to repurpose other antibiotics for the treatment of intracellular bacterial infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metronidazole
Drug delivery
Porphyromonas gingivalis
Nanomedicine
Carbon quantum dot

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Survival rate of intracellular P. Gingivalis
Morphological and structural characterization of cCQDs
Cytotoxicity and cell penetration by cCQDs
Evaluation of the intracellular antimicrobial activity of cCQDMET

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Australian Postgraduate Award (SMA and AD). The authors thank Dr. Donna Lai, Dr. Sheng Hua and Marry Simonian at the University of Sydney with biological experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of cCQD (inset: FFT with no visible diffraction); (B) Particle size distribution (scale bar: 50 nm).
The FTIR spectra of chlorophyll and cCQDs.
XPS spectra of cCQDs: (A) full survey spectrum, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum, and (D) O1s spectrum.
Optical properties of cCQDs: (A) UV–Vis spectrum, (B) PL spectra at variable wavelengths, (C) TPE spectra at variable wavelengths and (D) Lifetime decay profile.

Cell viability results based on LDH assays with exposure of up to 1000 µg/mL of cCQD to the cultured oral epithelial cells for 4 and 24-h compared with the control cells (equivalent to 100% at each given concentration). Error bars represent the standard deviation from two independent experiments.

Internalization of cCQDs into H413 cells after 4 h of incubation. (A) Nuclei stained with DAPI, (B) cCQDs in the cytoplasm in the green channel, (C) Weak emission in the red channel and (D) Overlay of A and B and C (scale bar: 20 µm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FTIR spectra confirming drug loading via esterification and hydrogen bonding (A) cCQDs and (B) cCQDMET after conjugation.
Antimicrobial activity of MET and cCQDMET against P. gingivalis.

Cellular uptake of cCQDMET with increasing fluorescence intensity of internalised cCQDMET over a 4-h period: (A) 1 h, (B) 2 h, (C) 3 h and (D) 4 h; (E) Mean fluorescence intensity of the internalized cCQD compared to control wells show plateauing over a 4-h period. (F) Using peak absorbance of metronidazole at 320 nm, the percentage uptake of cCQDMET compared to control wells also show plateauing over a 4-h period. Scale bar: 50 µm.

(A) Summary of average counting numbers of intracellular P. gingivalis colonies (CFU) from blood agar cultures to indicate the efficacy of cCQDMET conjugate; Legend: paired t-test (mean ± SD, two-tailed, 95% confidence interval) from at least 3 consecutive experiments. ** = (p ≤ 0.01) and *** = (p ≤ 0.001). Cells with intracellular P. gingivalis labelled with a specific antibody in the red channel: (B) treated with MET at 7 µM concentration, (C) treated with the conjugate at 7 µM concentration (MET-equivalent). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Synthesis of cCQD and drug conjugation.

head.ce:title: Conjugated carbon quantum dots: Potent nano-antibiotic for intracellular pathogens

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Dentistry, Faculty of Medicine and Health, The University of Sydney, Camperdown, NSW 2145, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Dentistry, Faculty of Medicine and Health, The University of Sydney, Camperdown, NSW 2145, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Dental Research, Westmead Centre for Oral Health, Westmead, NSW 2145, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Dental Research, Westmead Centre for Oral Health, Westmead, NSW 2145, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at.: School of Dentistry, Faculty of Medicine and Health, The University of Sydney, Camperdown, NSW 2145, Australia (K.-A. Nguyen); The University of Sydney, School of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia (V.G. Gomes).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A common theme in the persistence of microbial infections involves intracellular survival of microbial pathogens within host cells where they stay sheltered from attack by antimicrobial agents. In order to improve antimicrobial access inside host cells, we developed nanoparticles intracellular delivery of antibiotics. Using an intracellular infection model with the periodontal pathogen, Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis), we demonstrated significantly enhanced intracellular microbicidal activity with the standard antibiotic metronidazole (MET) through its conjugation onto 1–5 nm biocompatible nano-carrier, carbon quantum dot, which was derived from chlorophyll (cCQD). The conjugated cCQD-MET were rapidly internalized into the cultured cells, reaching almost 90% uptake within 3 h of the challenge. Our results consistently showed enhanced antimicrobial activity of the conjugate compared to MET alone. Even at concentrations as low as 0.26 µM, the conjugate showed 72% enhancement compared to the drug alone, resulting in significantly increased inhibition of intracellular P. gingivalis at lower antibiotic dosages. We achieved a high drug payload (80% w/w) on cCQD without affecting the potency of metronidazole as determined by cytotoxicity assays, cellular uptake of metronidazole, P. gingivalis invasion and elimination assays. The synthesized cCQD also displayed high fluorescence with 56% quantum yield at an absorbance peak of 380 nm and an emission peak of 480 nm, thus, allowing for fluorescence tracking and quantification of the drug intracellularly. A similar strategy may be used to repurpose other antibiotics for the treatment of intracellular bacterial infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Periodontal disease is a common, chronic inflammatory disease of the periodontium (gums around the teeth) affecting both humans and higher order mammals [1]. The disease is a consequence of inappropriate host immune response against a pathogenic biofilm accumulated around the teeth, culminating in the destruction of tooth-supporting tissues and eventual tooth loss [2]. More than 500 bacterial species have been reported to colonize the oral cavity, and a number of key species have been reported to be critical for the development of the disease. Studies have shown that Porphyromonas gingivalis, an anaerobic Gram-negative bacterium, to be one of the main etiological agents in the pathogenesis and progression of periodontal disease. General treatment strategies presently available involves regular removal of the pathogenic biofilm from the tooth surface to allow for the resolution of the inflammation. However, it is difficult to eliminate the disease and recurrence or progression of the disease is often encountered. Several issues contribute to disease relapse, and one of the key contributing factor includes the persistence of intracellular bacteria within the local host cells. The internalization of bacteria into host cells protects them from the host immune response and the action of the antibiotics [3,4].

In general, many antibiotics do not have good penetration into eukaryotic cells [5–7]. The tendency of drugs to move from areas of high concentration to regions of lower concentration (passive diffusion) is the force that drives most drugs to cross the plasma membrane. Lipid-soluble molecules are unionized and can translocate through the lipid bilayer readily, but the converse is true for water-soluble drugs [8]. Metronidazole, a commercially available and widely-used antibiotic is active against anaerobic bacteria. The mechanism of action of this antibiotic is through the reduction of its nitro-group by an electron transport protein in anaerobic bacteria, which can then cause strand damage to the bacterial DNA leading to cell death. However, metronidazole and other antibiotics used to treat periodontitis cannot readily cross the plasma membrane of host cells [5,9,10]. This necessitates multiple antibiotic treatments, which increases the risks of antibiotic resistance [11] via decreased uptake of the drug, altered nitro-reduction efficiency, overexpression of efflux pumps and inactivation of the drug and/or increased DNA repair [12,13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have proven to be effective in drug delivery mostly owing to their essential properties of biocompatibility [14–17] and non-bleaching photo-luminescence [18], enabling high cargo transport and long-term imaging and tracking [19,20]. Several studies have investigated the antibacterial activity of CQDs towards various microorganisms. Dou et al. synthesized CQDs from glucose, passivated them with branched (CDb) or linear (CDL) poly(ethyleneimine) and finally functionalized with benzyl bromide [21]. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) towards gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) was reported as 64 and 16 µg·mL−1 and gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus as 32 and 16 µg·mL−1, respectively for CDb and CDL. CQDs with variable surface charges were also investigated where positively charged CQDs showed anti-microbial activity towards E. coli [22]. A recent study reported the synthesis of metronidazole-derived CQDs and reported selectivity towards P. gingivalis [23]. However, no data were reported on the efficiency of this compound against intracellular bacteria, which are the primary sources of infection relapses [24].

Other groups have reported the synthesis of non-water soluble porphyrin-conjugated metronidazole or derivatives to deliver a high dose (160 µg/mL) of the antibiotic intracellularly to overcome the infection [10,25]. Herein, we propose a different approach. CQDs were synthesized from chlorophyll in a controlled hydrothermal reactor to act solely as a drug carrier (scheme 1 ). The size of the nanoparticles was controlled by two-step dialysis to ensure the uniformity of CQD size and that metronidazole was loaded on the surface of CQDs by electrostatic interactions and bonding via esterification (cCQDMET). This compound was then tested to verify the potency of the loaded drug. By taking advantage of the biocompatibility of the CQDs, conjugated metronidazole was successfully delivered into the cells for improved pharmacological activity compared to metronidazole alone.

All chemicals used were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Australia). Dialysis membranes were purchased from Spectra/Pro Biotech (Australia), and the P. gingivalis (ATCC 33277 strain) was procured from ATCC (USA). The epithelial cell line (H413) was derived from a human oral squamous cell carcinoma.

Chlorophyll (2.0 g) was dispersed in 70 mL deionized water and placed in a hydrothermal reactor at 240 °C for 18 h. The final product was collected and centrifuged at 5000g for 30 min. The supernatant was removed and passed through a 0.22 µm disposable cellulose acetate syringe filter unit (Advantec, Qld, Australia). Two sets of Amicon ultrafiltration membranes (Merck, Australia) with 3 kDa and 30 kDa molecular-weight cut-offs (MWCO) were used to obtain nanoparticles with the molecular weight (MW) between 3 and 30 kDa (average MW of 16.5 kDa was used for subsequent molar calculations). The stock solution with a quantum yield of 56% was lyophilized, denoted as chlorophyll carbon quantum dots (cCQD) and kept in a desiccator for further analysis.

The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized cCQDs was measured by a standard comparison method with respect to a reference material [26]. Quinine sulfate with a known QY of 0.5421 was used as a reference sample, and the main samples containing cCQDs were used after diluting with deionized water (DI water, pH 7.0) to aliquots of variable concentrations. For each sample, the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength was recorded. Then, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the solution was measured and the integrated PL intensity was calculated [27]. The procedures were repeated at varying concentrations, and the integrated PL intensity versus absorbance data were analyzed.

The QY values were estimated using Eq. (1), where “ϕ” is the QY, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “η” is the refractive index of the solvent, and “A” is the optical density. The subscripts “st” and “x” refer to values that correspond to the standard sample with known QY and the cCQD sample, respectively [28]. The excitation/emission slit parameters were kept constant throughout the PL measurements.
(1)
ϕ x
=
ϕ
st
(
I x
/
I
st
)
(
n x 2
/
n
st
2
)
(
A
st
/
A x
)

JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV was used to study the size and particle size distribution of cCQDs. The synthesized carbon nanomaterials were placed on a 200 mesh Cu grid (coated with a carbon film and dried in a vacuum desiccator) [29], and particle size distribution was determined using ImageJ (NIH, USA). The percent transmission and surface state data were obtained by using Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured with the Cary60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The fluorescence spectra with variable excitation wavelengths were performed using a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, USA).

Two-photon excitation/emission spectra and the PL lifetime decay profiles were evaluated using a Leica SP5 II confocal microscope equipped with a FLIM system (Becker and Hickl, SPC830, TCSPC) having 256 × 256-pixel resolution and 256-time channels with a 25× water immersion objective. A 780 nm picosecond laser with 40 MHz repetition rate was used as the excitation source for the lifetime decay profile of cCQDs. A mercury lamp/Mi Tai pulsed laser with adjustable excitation wavelength was used as the light source. The chemical composition of the cCQDs was investigated via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi USA) using a monochromatic Al-K X-ray source (h = 1486.68 eV).

Equal volumes of 0.5 mg/mL of metronidazole (MET) and cCQD were mixed in distilled water with stirring (400 rpm, 25 °C) for 2 h in the absence of light. Unbound MET was removed from the mixture by centrifugation with Amicon ultracentrifuge filter cartridges with the molecular cut-off 3 kDa for 20 min at 5000g to obtain the conjugated cCQD bound to MET (denoted as cCQDMET). The Beer-Lambert law was used to quantify the drug loading capacity of the nanoparticles. A UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Cary60, Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to measure the loading capacity of cCQDs, by comparing the cCQDMET absorption peak with the MET standard curve at absorbance peak of MET at 320 nm (based on 5 different concentrations of cCQDs). The drug loading was calculated according to the following equation:
D r u g
l o a d i n g
%
=
M a s s
o f
M E T
i n
c C Q D
M E T
M a s s
o f
M E T
i n
f e e d
∗ 100

The final conjugated product contained 0.5 mg/mL of cCQDs with 0.4 mg/mL of conjugated MET (Fig. S1), equivalent to 2333 µM of metronidazole. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to verify the conjugation of MET and cCQDs through esterification.

The epithelial cell line (H413), derived from a human oral squamous cell carcinoma, was used for all cell culture work in this study. H413 cells exhibit both characteristic epithelial morphology and high CD24 expression typical of the sulcular epithelium observed in periodontal disease [10]. Cells were cultured in Eagle’s Minimum Essential Medium (MEM; Sigma Aldrich, Castle Hill, NSW, Australia) and 10% fetal calf serum (FCS; CSL Limited, Parkville, VIC, Australia) at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere. Cells were harvested with TrypLE Express (replacement for 0.05% trypsin/ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, Invitrogen, Mulgrave, VIC, Australia) and passaged every 3 days. Porphyromonas gingivalis (strain ATCC 33277) was maintained in enriched brain heart infusion (BHI) medium (per liter: 37 g BHI, 5 g yeast extract, 0.5 g L-cysteine·HCl, 5 mg hemin and supplemented with 2 mg menadione broth) in an anaerobic chamber (Don Whitley Scientific, Australia) at 37 °C with an atmosphere of 80% N2, 10% CO2, and 10% H2. Bacterial numbers were estimated by reference to a standard curve determined by optical absorbance at 600 nm OD = 0.8 is equivalent to 1 × 109 mL−1.

For determining cellular cytotoxicity in response to cCQDs, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay was used to evaluate the cell membrane integrity using the Pierce LDH cytotoxicity assay kit (Thermo Scientific, Australia). Briefly, H413 cells were harvested and plated in 96-well flat bottom plates at 10,000 cells per well in 100 μL MEM medium and cultured in an incubator at 37 °C, 5% CO2 overnight and then challenged with 10 μL of varying final concentrations of cCQDs (200 to 1000 µg/mL) in triplicate. At 4 and 24 h, the culture supernatant was collected for LDH quantification along with maximum LDH activity controls (cells lysed with supplied Lysis Buffer) or medium alone with and without cells were used as background controls [10]. 50 μL of each sample was mixed with 50 μL of the reaction mixture in a 96-well plate and incubated at room temperature for 30 min in the dark. Subsequently, 50 μL of Stop Solution was added to each well, and the optical absorbance was read at 490 nm and 680 nm (SpectraMax i3, Molecular Devices, USA). In order to determine the LDH activity, the 680 nm absorbance value (background) was subtracted from the 490 nm absorbance before calculating %Cytotoxicity [(LDH at 490 nm) − (LDH at 680 nm)].
% C y t o t o x i c i t y = ( C Q D s
t r e a t e d
L D H - c o n t r o l ) / ( M a x
L D H - c o n t r o l )
∗
100

To evaluate the antibacterial activity of the cCQDs and the potency of the cCQDMET, a two-fold serial dilution of samples was carried out using the broth as the diluent in a 96-well plate. A late exponential phase starter culture was used to prepare an inoculum stock (OD600 = 0.05) in the enriched BHI medium. The highest concentration of 160 μM MET-equivalent of cCQDMET was added in triplicate to the inoculum stock to a final volume of 100 µL and serially diluted 12 times with dilution factor of two. P. gingivalis-only was used as a positive control and cCQD, MET and the cCQDMET without bacteria was used as background controls. The 96-well plate was incubated in the anaerobic chamber for 48 h before the growth was assessed at OD600 using SpectraMax i3 microplate platform (Molecular Devices, USA). The curve fitting was accomplished using GraphPad Prism (v.7.0) software to determine the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) for each condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Survival rate of intracellular P. Gingivalis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effectiveness of the cCQDMET against the intracellular P. gingivalis, 1.0 × 105 H413 cells were seeded in 12-well cell culture plates and grown to confluence and challenged with P. gingivalis at a multiplicity of infection of 100 bacteria/epithelial cells. The bacteria were then allowed to invade the cells for 1.5 h at 37 °C in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air. To kill extracellular bacteria, cells were treated with 200 and 300 µg/mL of metronidazole and gentamycin, respectively, for 1 h and subsequently washed with PBS three times. Cells were then treated with the serial dilution of cCQDMET and MET separately, starting from 21.4 µM MET-equivalent concentration with the dilution factor of 3 in 1.0 mL MEM medium containing 10% FBS. Fresh medium without any cCQDMET/MET was used as controls. After incubation for 3 h, the media was discarded, and the cells were washed with PBS three times before being harvested by scraping and were lysed with sterile distilled water (200 µL for 15 min on ice). Lysates were immediately transferred to an anaerobic chamber with serial plating onto enriched BHI + 4% sheep's blood agar plates. Plates were incubated for 2 days before the number of colonies were enumerated to determine the intracellular survival rate of P. gingivalis.

For cell imaging experiments, 5 × 104 cells/well oral epithelial cells (H413) were seeded in 6-well sterile Ibidi chamber slides (Ibidi, Vic, Australia) in 300 μL MEM Complete Medium. Following overnight incubation, the media was replaced with a media containing 500 μg/mL cCQDs and incubated for a further 24 h [30]. Subsequently, cells were washed three times with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 1 h and washed with PBS. Slides were mounted with ProLong Gold antifade reagent with DAPI (Molecular Probes, Invitrogen, USA) and the images were recorded by a Leica SP5 II confocal microscope with mercury lamp as the light source.

For the colocalization of cCQDMET and P. gingivalis, 300 µL H413 cell suspension (6 × 104 cells/mL) were seeded in an 8-well open µ-Slide (chambered coverslip) and incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 3 days. The medium was changed every day. After 3 days, the cells were treated with cCQDMET for 3 h. Subsequently, cells were washed for 5 min thrice with PBS followed by fixing with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 1 h and washed thrice again with PBS and permeabilized for 15 min with 0.1% Triton-X100/PBS. After washing twice with PBS, 4% PFA was added for another 5 min followed by washing twice with PBS, incubation with 100 µL of 0.2% glycine/PBS for 10 min and rinsing with PBS. Blocking buffer was added for 1 h and rinsed with PBS followed by probing with mouse monoclonal antibody IIB2 [31,32] specific for gingipains as a primary antibody (5 μg·mL−1) for 1 h at 37 °C. Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated rabbit anti-mouse secondary antibody (1:100 dilution, Invitrogen) was added and incubated for an additional hour before the cells were washed and visualized with a Leica SP5 II confocal microscope.

For determining the cCQDMET uptake, the oral epithelial cells were cultured on MatTek glass bottom dishes until ∼100% confluency was reached. The cells were washed with PBS and 160 µM of the cCQDMET (MET-equivalent) in a final volume of 1.0 mL of media was added to each well. The media was removed at intervals of 1, 2, 3 and 4 h. The cells were washed with 1.0 mL of PBS three times and imaged with a Leica SP5 II confocal microscope (excitation wavelength of 780 nm multi-photon).

In the second set of experiments, the amount of MET internalized into the cells were quantified using MET absorbance peak at 320 nm. Briefly, cells were cultured in a 12-well plate until ∼100% confluency was reached. The same procedure as explained in the previous paragraph was repeated and the cells were harvested by scraping and lysing with sterile 0.5 mL distilled water for 15 min on ice. 0.5 mL of 100 mM HEPES, pH 7 was then added to each well and incubated for 15 min. Absorption intensity at 320 nm was read using a CLARIOstar spectrometer (BMG LABTECH, USA) to determine the quantity of cCQDMET uptake intracellularly [33]. Samples with MET and cells only were used as positive and negative controls respectively. The absorbance intensity at 320 was subtracted from the results to calculate the uptake (%) of the cCQDMET by cells. All experiments ran in triplicate.

GraphPad Prism (v7.0) software was used for paired t-test (mean ± SD., two-tailed, 95% confidence interval range) analyses using at least three consecutive experiments. P-values of <0.05 were considered significant for the statistical analysis. ImageJ (NIH, USA) software was used for bacterial counting from the optical images, and the particle size distribution was estimated using images from JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of cCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The images obtained from the TEM showed spherical morphology for cCQD. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) diffraction pattern showed no visible diffraction (Fig. 1 A), indicating that the particles are amorphous with sizes ranging from 2 to 4 nm (Fig. 1B). The narrow particle size distribution achieved here ensures a more consistent ingress of cCQDs into cells and increases the nanoparticle internalization to deliver MET intracellularly. Similar findings were previously reported for gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), where smaller particles (2–6 nm) showed deeper penetration into tumor tissues compared to larger particles (6–15 nm) [34].

The FTIR results show the common characteristic peaks of cCQDs (COC, CO, CN, CH, NH, and OH) in Fig. 2 . The peak at 1600 cm−1 can be ascribed to the carboxyl group and the peak at 1195 cm−1 attributed to CO [26,27,35]. The carboxyl group present on cCQD can potentially react with the OH group of MET to form an ester for drug loading. The peak starting from 1400 to 1500 cm−1 is assigned to CN groups [36]. The stretching vibration of the CH bond at 2888 cm−1 is noticeable [27,37,38].

Fig. 3 shows the results from XPS analysis to characterize the chemical composition of cCQDs. We note four dominant peaks for C 1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O 1s (531 eV) and a minor peak for chlorine (197.5 eV). The ratio of carbon to nitrogen was determined to be 70.09:10.28. It has been previously observed that high nitrogen content typically correlates with a high quantum yield of carbon quantum dots [39]. In the high-resolution spectra (Fig. 3B), the C 1s envelope can be deconvoluted into four peaks, corresponding to sp2 carbons (CC, 284.8 eV), sp3 carbons (CO/CN, 286.0 eV), carbonyl carbons (CO, 287.8 eV), and carboxyl carbons (COOH, 288.7 eV). The strong signal of CC coordination is ascribed to the predominantly carbonized character of this material [40]. The N1s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.7 and 401.3 eV indicating an enormous contribution of nitrogen in two different coordination states namely CN and NH bonds respectively. The O1s band contains two peaks at 531.5 and 532.8 eV for CO and CO, respectively [27,39].

The UV–Vis spectrum of the sample shows two prominent peaks at 390 nm and 488 nm respectively, which supports the notion that the optical absorption of the cCQDs is dominated by the organic surface functional moieties (n-π* transition). As shown in Fig. 4 A, the optical absorption spectrum of cCQDs is made up of multiple electronic transitions distributed over the absorption wavelengths with their absorption profiles being superpositions of contributions derived from various state transitions in which the more strongly absorptive species co-exist in the green and blue regions of the visible spectrum.

The fluorescence spectra of the cCQDs on probing with a series of excitation wavelengths are shown in Figs. 4B and S2. The emissions are excitation wavelength dependent and the peak fluorescence band shifts progressively with changes in excitation towards longer wavelengths, while decreasing in emission intensities after a peak maximum of about 400 nm. These changes in emission color as a function of excitation wavelength are commonly observed for CQDs and likely reflect the availability of different surface traps at different energies varying from dot to dot as a consequence of structure inhomogeneity [41]. However, a closer inspection of the fluorescence spectra reveals a change in the shape of the emission spectra as the excitation wavelength increases towards the red region of the visible spectrum.

Three distinct regions in the FL spectra are identified (Fig. S2), when the cCQDs are excited from: (1) 300 nm to 380 nm, the emission band is broad and shows a single peak, (2) 400 nm to 440 nm, a sharp drop in emission intensity is observed with two peaks identified in each emission band, and (3) 460 nm to 500 nm, the FL band shows a single peak again. This complex behavior arises from the existence of the two absorption peaks (shown as A and B in the UV–Vis spectrum). In the first region, only the emission center “A” is active, and only one peak is identified in the emission spectra whereas in the second region, both centers (A and B) are active, resulting in two peaks in the emission spectra. As the excitation wavelength progresses towards the red region of the visible spectrum, only the emission center “B” remains active, and the emission bands only show a single peak again.

Multi-Photon microscopy, a form of laser scanning microscopy, uses localized non-linear excitation to induce fluorescence. This technique is generally coupled with near-infrared (NIR) laser excitation to take advantage of low scattering, higher irradiation penetration and reduced photobleaching and tissue damage [42,43]. To understand the two-photon luminescence properties of cCQDs, the nanoparticles were excited at variable wavelengths from 740 to 900 nm using a femtosecond pulsed laser (Fig. 4C). Care was taken to ensure that the laser output power remained constant at all wavelengths during the experiment.

The spectra in Fig. 4C shows that the two-photon emission intensity is strongest at
λ
ex = 760 nm with
λ
em = 460 nm. A similar trend was observed for the single-photon excitation. At lower excitation wavelengths (740 and 760 nm) a main peak at higher energy (460 nm) and a shoulder at lower energies (510 nm) is detected. However, as the excitation wavelength increases (780–820 nm), the relative intensity of the two peaks change considerably. As mentioned for the PL analysis of the sample, the two emission centers are excited at different wavelengths and both remain active during the two-photon excitation experiment. The final emission intensity is the sum of the emission intensities originating from each center.

Fig. 4D shows the fluorescence lifetime decay, which was fitted satisfactorily using a double-exponential function with lifetimes of τ1 = 12.27 ns, τ2 = 4.85 ns. These indicate that the luminescence is dominated by contributions from the surface functional groups and carbogenic core respectively [26].

The dependence of PL intensity to the chemical environment was measured by plotting the relationship between variable pH and emission intensity of cCQD (Fig. S3). A lower emission intensity observed for pH 6 and 8 compared to that for pH 7 could be due to the higher amount of H+ or OH− ions which lead to disruption or even prohibition of the electronic transitions of the defects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell penetration by cCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to use of cCQDs for drug conjugation and cell imaging applications, we measured the cytotoxicity via lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay of cCQDs in cell culture with variable concentrations starting from 200 µg/mL to 1000 µg/mL. The cytoplasmic LDH is gradually released into the culture medium as the cell membrane integrity becomes compromised upon exposure to toxic compounds. As shown in Fig. 5 , the cCQDs do not show any significant cytotoxicity even at concentrations as high as 1000 µg/mL after 4- and 24-h of incubation. Thus, the biocompatibility of cCQDs makes them an excellent choice for cell imaging, tracking, and intracellular drug delivery.

To examine the ability of the cCQDs to penetrate the cell cytoplasm and attack intracellular pathogens, we cultivated oral epithelial cells (H413) with cCQDs for 4 h at 37 °C. The nuclei were stained with DAPI (Fig. 6 A), and a strong emission was detected in the green channel (Fig. 6B) when cCQDs were excited at 488 nm while almost no signal was detected in the red channel suggesting that cCQDs were internalized (Fig. 6C).

The antibiotic metronidazole (MET) was conjugated onto cCQDs, and the drug loading capacity was found to be 80% (w/w) using UV–Vis spectroscopy. The resulting conjugate has been denoted as cCQDMET. Based on the MET and cCQD molecular weights and relative drug loading capacity, the average molar ratio of cCQDs to MET in the cCQDMET was calculated to be 1:77. The Fischer esterification reaction and hydrogen bonding were confirmed to be the primary mechanisms of drug loading using FTIR spectroscopy. In summary, the carboxylic acid moiety of cCQDs reacted with the alcohol group on the MET to form an ester. As can be seen in Fig. 7 , the Fischer esterification reaction is clear at 1600 cm−1 (contributed by ester CO stretch) with the slight broadening of the peak, accommodating both CO and ester bonds at the same wavenumber. We also note the presence of hydrogen bonding at around 3200 to 3550 cm−1.

To assess whether the process of MET conjugation onto cCQD had an impact on the potency of MET with respect to its bactericidal activity, the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) and half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of cCQDMET were compared with those for MET alone. It was found that the conjugation of MET onto cCQD had no deleterious effect on the activity of MET itself since the IC50 of cCQDMET was 0.33 µM vs. IC50 of MET at 1.02 µM. Hence, a 3-fold enhanced performance was observed with cCQDMET. With respect to stability of cCQDMET, the conjugate was found to retain its potency even after storing it in DI water in the fridge for six months.

In a recent study by Liu et al. [23], the antibacterial properties of a MET-derived CQD showed selectivity towards P. gingivalis and did not show any effect on Streptococcus mutans (S. mutans) or Escherichia coli (E. coli) as these latter species are not anaerobes. Though CQDs could inhibit the bacterial growth (71.7% at 1.25 µg·mL−1), the CQDs were not as effective as MET at any given concentration. In this study, at a concentration of 1.25 µg. mL−1 (7.0 µM MET equivalent), the cCQDMET showed 5.5 times greater efficiency towards P. gingivalis compared to MET itself, as shown in Fig. 8 (semi-log plot).

A reservoir of intracellular bacteria within oral epithelial cells may contribute towards the recurrence of microbial dysbiosis in periodontal disease. Intracellular localization of bacteria protects them from the host immune system surveillance and the action of antibiotics which could be at a sub-therapeutic concentration within the cell's intracellular compartments [5]. To assess the degree of drug internalization by cCQDMET and into the epithelial cells over time, two sets of experiments were conducted by exploiting the fluorescence property of cCQDMET. At regular time intervals, non-internalized cCQDMET were removed by washing with PBS. Then confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to estimate the percentage of cCQDMET uptake by measuring the mean fluorescence intensity of the remaining cCQDMET within the cells. We found that cCQDMET internalization reached equilibrium at 90% of total cCQDMET after approximately 3–4 h of incubation (Fig. 9 A–E).

In the second set of experiments, using the MET absorbance peak at 320 nm, the amount of internalized cCQDMET was quantified at various time intervals. Similar to the cCQD uptake assay above, the level of internalized cCQDMET was confirmed to reach equilibrium at approximately 90% of total cCQDMET after 3–4 h of incubation (Fig. 9F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the intracellular antimicrobial activity of cCQDMET
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Fig. 10 A, over the entire range of concentrations tested, cCQDMET shows higher antimicrobial response and a significant increase in terms of inhibition for the intracellular P. gingivalis compared with MET alone. Even at 0.26 µM (MET-equivalent), the percent inhibition is 72% higher for the conjugate as compared to metronidazole. The reason for this enhanced performance could be the availability of a higher dosage of intracellular MET because of the ability of cCQDMET to successfully transport multiple MET molecules intracellularly, potentially overwhelming the efflux pumps, which would otherwise pump the drug out of the cells. To verify that MET remained attached to cCQDs, cells were incubated with the conjugate for 4 h followed by cell lysis and dialysis using a 3.0 kDa cut-off (since our cCQD particle sizes are larger than 3 kDa and MET is only 171 Da, this step ensures only unconjugated MET passes through the membrane and are removed). Metronidazole release was quantified using MET’s UV–Vis absorption spectrum at 320 nm. No peak was observed in the spectra of the dialysate, showing that the drug remained attached to the cCQDs.

Similar enhanced performance of antimicrobial activity was reported by Wayakanon et al. where poly[2-(methacryloyloxy) ethyl phosphorylcholine] (PMPC)- poly[2-(diisopropylamino) ethyl methacrylate] (PDPA) polymersome were used to deliver and burst release metronidazole intracellularly into H357, NOK, and TR146 cells to kill P. gingivalis (strain A245br) [9]. They reported that the encapsulated MET (29.2 µM) reduced the number of intracellular P. gingivalis by more than half (21 × 104 to 8 × 104 CFU/mL) in H357 cells. The number of colonies decreased by 33% in the case of NOK cells, while intracellular bacteria in TR146 were least affected. Mugabe et al. also reported the antimicrobial efficiency of liposome-entrapped aminoglycosides and free aminoglycosides against resistant clinical strains of P. aeruginosa and reported a significant decrease in minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) from ≥32 µg/mL to ≤8 µg/mL [44]. In our study, the number of bacteria surviving intracellularly decreased by approximately 99% when 21 µM of the cCQDMET (MET-equivalent) was used.

Ye et al. reported the use of porphyrin linked MET (Por-MET) to target intracellular P. gingivalis. Por-MET was not water soluble, and DMSO was used as a solvent [10]. In their experiment, 40 µM of Por-MET showed almost full inhibition against intracellular P. gingivalis, which we were able to achieve using only 21.40 µM cCQDMET; hence, about half the dosage with a significantly less complex synthesis.

To visually confirm the enhanced antimicrobial activity of cCQDMET, confocal microscopy was performed on infected cells treated with MET and separately with cCQDMET at a concentration of 7.10 µM (MET-equivalent). The mouse monoclonal antibody IIB2 [31,32] specific for gingipains of P. gingivalis and Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated rabbit anti-mouse was used as a primary and a secondary antibody, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10B, after 3 h of treatment, abundant intracellular bacteria remained visible within H413 cells when incubated with MET, while treatment with cCQDMET succeeded in eliminating almost 100% of the intracellular bacteria (Fig. 10C). These results are consistent with those for the viability assays shown in Fig. 10A.

This work outlines the synthesis and deployment of a nano-antibiotic conjugate derived from chlorophyll to serve as a nanocarrier (cCQD) for the delivery of the widely used metronidazole drug. A reproducible approach was developed to prepare the conjugated cCQDMET having a drug loading capacity of 80% w/w (MET). Our results showed that at 21.4 µM of MET equivalent, the bacterial viability was calculated to be 18.6% and 0.4% for the bare drug and the conjugate respectively. This significant enhancement is due to the efficient penetration and delivery of the cCQDMET through the cell cytoplasm and into the epithelial cells, thereby significantly increasing the local concentration of MET inside the cells and decreasing the number of colonies by 99%. Such formulations can be used for eliminating P. gingivalis from the oral cavity. After 3 h of incubation, 90% of the drug was internalized by the cells. In all serial dilutions of the conjugate and the drug, cCQDMET performance was significantly superior. At the lowest concentration tested (0.26 µM MET equivalent), the conjugate inhibited P. gingivalis growth by 72% over that of MET alone. In a previous study, a porphyrin conjugated MET, developed through complicated chemistry [25], reported an effective concentration of 40 µg/mL compared to 7 µg/mL in this work. Thus, our results show that about six times lower dosage is effective when MET is conjugated via a facile one-step synthesis process to produce a water-soluble, fluorescent compound which enables tracking and imaging. The developed cCQD-antibiotic therapy is suitable for use as a smart drug delivery system for relevant intracellular infections with a lower dosage, and possibly reduced side effects and lower risk for antibiotic resistance.

We gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Australian Postgraduate Award (SMA and AD). The authors thank Dr. Donna Lai, Dr. Sheng Hua and Marry Simonian at the University of Sydney with biological experiments.
The authors have no conflict of interest to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.05.067.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
